questionText: Let $n$ be an integer. Determine whether the statement '$n$ is a multiple of $5$' is a sufficient condition for '$n^2$ is a multiple of $5$'. Justify your answer with algebra.

steps.workingLatex: P:\ n \text{ is a multiple of } 5, \qquad Q:\ n^2 \text{ is a multiple of } 5
steps.explanation: Labelling the conditions keeps the argument organised. P is a claim about $n$; Q is a claim about $n^2$.

steps.description: State what needs to be shown.
steps.workingLatex: P \text{ sufficient for } Q \iff P \Rightarrow Q
steps.explanation: P is sufficient for Q precisely when assuming P lets us deduce Q. So we assume $n$ is a multiple of $5$ and try to reach a statement about $n^2$.
Turning 'multiple of $5$' into $n=5k$ converts the wording into algebra we can manipulate. The letter $k$ stands for whatever integer makes this true.
Squaring is the natural move because Q is a statement about $n^2$. This expresses $n^2$ directly in terms of the integer $k$.

steps.description: Factor out 5 to reveal the multiple.
steps.explanation: Pulling out a factor of $5$ shows $n^2$ as $5$ times an integer, since $5k^2$ is an integer. That is exactly the form 'multiple of $5$' requires.

steps.workingLatex: n^2 = 5\,(5k^2) \;\Rightarrow\; Q \text{ is true}, \quad \text{so } P \Rightarrow Q
steps.explanation: We started from P and derived Q with no gaps, so the forward implication is proven. Being a multiple of $5$ guarantees the square is a multiple of $5$.

steps.workingLatex: P \text{ is a sufficient condition for } Q
steps.explanation: Since $P \Rightarrow Q$ was established directly, P is sufficient for Q. The clean factorisation is what makes the guarantee watertight.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: Yes, it is sufficient. If $n = 5k$ then $n^2 = 25k^2 = 5(5k^2)$, which is a multiple of $5$; hence $P \Rightarrow Q$.
